findings.title: Recall registration happens after the message is already enqueued
findings.description: `send_message` clones `msg.cancelled`, calls `queue_tx.try_send(msg)`, and only then inserts the flag into `pending_recalls`. Because the worker can dequeue immediately, it can run the new `pending_recalls.remove(&msg.message_id)` path before this insert happens. That leaves a stale recall entry for a message that has already been injected into Claude, so `/Recall` can later return success even though the message was already sent.
findings.suggestion: Register the recall handle before making the message visible to the worker. For example, insert into `pending_recalls` first, then enqueue, and roll the map entry back if `try_send` fails. Alternatively, move recall bookkeeping into the queued item/state protected by the same mutex as the queue operation.

findings.title: Cancellation uses `Ordering::Relaxed`, so the worker is not guaranteed to observe recalls
findings.description: `try_recall` stores `true` with `Ordering::Relaxed`, while the worker checks `msg.cancelled.load(Ordering::Relaxed)` in multiple dequeue sites. This feature relies on one task publishing a cancellation and another task observing it before injecting the message into stdin. With only relaxed operations, there is no acquire/release edge for that handoff, so on weaker memory models the worker can still observe `false` and forward the message.
findings.suggestion: Use `cancelled.store(true, Ordering::Release)` and `cancelled.load(Ordering::Acquire)` at every worker check, or avoid the atomic entirely and keep cancellation state under the same mutex/channel that controls queue visibility.

findings.title: `pending_recalls` entries are never cleaned up when a session is aborted or evicted
findings.description: The new global `pending_recalls` map is populated for queued messages, but the added code does not remove those entries when a session is killed, evicted for LRU, swept as idle, or when the worker exits with messages still buffered. After teardown, `try_recall` can still find and 'recall' a message that will never reach Claude because the session is already gone, and the map can grow with stale entries over time.
findings.suggestion: Tie pending recalls to session lifetime and clear them during every teardown path (`kill_session`, eviction, idle sweep, worker exit), or store pending message IDs inside `SessionInner` so they are dropped with the session.

summary: The recall feature has two real race conditions and a teardown leak: registration happens too late, the atomic ordering is too weak for cross-task cancellation, and stale recall entries survive session shutdown.
